title: MiFID II Algo Trading — Kill-Switch Activation Audit Trail and Dual Authorization

compliance.assurance_rationale: MiFID II Art.17 — UC provides continuous Splunk telemetry evidence for this regulatory requirement, uplifted from contributing to partial after review confirmed the SPL captures relevant monitoring signals.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that MiFID II Art.17 (Algorithmic trading controls) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.5.10: MiFID II Algo Trading — Kill-Switch Activation Audit Trail and Dual Authorization.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.5.10' running on sourcetype oms:killswitch and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), OMS audit via HEC
spl:
index=trading sourcetype="oms:killswitch" action="FIRE" earliest=-400d
| eval need_second=if(require_second_approver IN ("true","1","yes"),1,0)
| where need_second=1 AND (isnull(second_actor) OR second_actor="")
| table _time, event_id, strategy_group, actor, second_actor, require_second_approver
description: Kill-switches must be reliable and controlled. Logging who armed, who confirmed, and which child strategies halted proves the firm can stop propagation of erroneous orders—a core supervisory expectation for algorithmic traders.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for MiFID II compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes trading and sourcetypes oms:killswitch. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Require OMS to emit one immutable row per `FIRE` with `require_second_approver` populated from policy; (2) integrate CyberArk or ServiceNow approval id as `second_actor` where applicable; (3) page markets compliance on any hit; (4) exclude dry-run environments via `index` or `host` filter; (5) map to RTS 6 and internal algo register identifiers. Indexes required: `index=trading`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=oms:killswitch`.
visualization: Table (policy breaches), Timeline (`_time` by event_id), Single value (count).
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), OMS audit via HEC.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=trading` `sourcetype="oms:killswitch"` (event_id, actor, action, strategy_group, require_second_approver, second_actor, _time).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Require OMS to emit one immutable row per `FIRE` with `require_second_approver` populated from policy; (2) integrate CyberArk or ServiceNow approval id as `second_actor` where applicable; (3) page markets compliance on any hit; (4) exclude dry-run environments via `index` or `host` filter; (5) map to RTS 6 and internal algo register identifiers.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=trading sourcetype="oms:killswitch" action="FIRE" earliest=-400d
| eval need_second=if(require_second_approver IN ("true","1","yes"),1,0)
| where need_second=1 AND (isnull(second_actor) OR second_actor="")
| table _time, event_id, strategy_group, actor, second_actor, require_second_approver
```

Understanding this SPL

**MiFID II Algo Trading — Kill-Switch Activation Audit Trail and Dual Authorization** — Kill-switches must be reliable and controlled. Logging who armed, who confirmed, and which child strategies halted proves the firm can stop propagation of erroneous orders—a core supervisory expectation for algorithmic traders.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=trading` `sourcetype="oms:killswitch"` (event_id, actor, action, strategy_group, require_second_approver, second_actor, _time). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), OMS audit via HEC. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: trading; **sourcetype**: oms:killswitch. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=trading, sourcetype="oms:killswitch", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **need_second** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where need_second=1 AND (isnull(second_actor) OR second_actor="")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Output fields: _time, actor, event_id, need_second, require_second_approver, second_actor, strategy_group.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (policy breaches), Timeline (`_time` by event_id), Single value (count).
grandmaExplanation: We watch for kill-switches must be reliable and controlled. logging who armed, who confirmed, and which child strategies halted proves the firm can stop propagation of erroneous orders—a core supervisory expect. This is critical because gaps here could mean serious regulatory exposure or security blind spots.
